title: BlackRock Now Controls Bitcoin's Future
transcript: Imagine  waking  up  to  find  that  the   fundamental  rule  of  Bitcoin  was  about  to   be  completely  rewritten.  Imagine  a   handful  of  developers  altering  the   blockchain's  history  to  recover  billions   in  lost  funds.  It  sounds  like  a   dystopian  nightmare.   >> Dystopian  nightmare   >> concocted  by  a  central  bank.  But  on  the   27th  of  February,  this  exact  scenario   was  proposed  for  the  world's  largest   cryptocurrency.  It  threatened  to  shatter   the  illusion  of  absolute  immutability   and  split  the  entire  network  in  two.   Wow.  My  name  is  Lewis  and  you're   watching  the  Coin  Bureau.   So,  is  the  digital  gold  thesis   officially  vulnerable  to  human   intervention?  Are  we  staring  down  the   barrel  of  a  chainsplit  doom  loop?  Or  is   Bitcoin  social  consensus  actually   stronger  than  ever?  That's  exactly  what   we're  going  to  cover  today.  First  things   first  though,  let's  look  at  the  catalyst   that  just  sent  a  shock  wave  through  the   Bitcoin  governance  model.  On  Friday  the   27th  of  February  2026,  former  Mount  Gox   CEO  Mark  Capellis  published  a  highly   controversial  pull  request  on  the   Bitcoin  core  GitHub.  He  proposed  a  hard   fork  to  recover  funds  from  one  of  the   most  infamous  wallets  in  crypto  history.   The  address  is  known  as  1  FEX.  According   to  onchain  data,  this  wallet  holds   exactly  79,957.22   BTC.  These  coins  were  stolen  during  a   compromise  of  Mount  Gaus's  internal   systems  back  in  the  1st  of  March  of   2011.  They  have  been  sitting  completely   dormant  for  15  years.  Because  the   private  keys  are  either  lost  or  held  by   the  original  attacker,  these  funds  are   effectively  frozen  in  plain  sight.  But   at  today's  prices,  that  trapped  Bitcoin   is  worth  approximately  $5.29  billion.   That  is  a  massive  pool  of  capital   sitting  completely  out  of  reach  of  the   Mount  Gox  creditors.  So,  Corpellis   proposed  a  patch  consisting  of  fewer   than  50  lines  of  code.  This  code  would   implement  a  consensus  rule  change.  Put   simply,  it  would  allow  those  specific   coins  to  be  spent  using  a  new  designated   recovery  signature  overriding  the   original  private  key.  It  was  a  direct   proposal  to  bypass  the  cryptographic   rules  of  Bitcoin  to  correct  a  historic   theft  and  the  market  implications  of   this  are  absolutely  terrifying.  Now,  to   understand  why  this  is  so  dangerous,  you   need  to  understand  the  doom  loop  that   lives  inside  a  contentious  hard  fork.  If   a  proposal  like  this  actually  gains   traction,  it  requires  near  unanimous   agreement  from  miners,  node  operators,   and  exchanges.  If  parts  of  the  ecosystem   refuse  to  adopt  the  rule  change,  the   network  fractures  and  you  get  a  split   chain.  One  chain  enforces  the  recovery   rule  while  the  other  preserves  the   original  ledger.  We  saw  exactly  how   destructive  this  can  be  during  the   Bitcoin  cash  split  in  2017  and  the   brutal  hash  wars  of  2018.  But  the  crypto   market  of  2026  is  vastly  more  leveraged   and  interconnected  than  it  was  back   then.  If  a  split  looks  imminent,  the   first  thing  major  exchanges  do  is  freeze   deposits  and  withdrawals  to  prevent   replay  attacks.  When  exchanges  freeze,   market  makers  pull  their  liquidity.   Order  books  thin  out  by  80  to  90%.  And   this  is  when  the  derivatives  market   implodes.  Just  look  at  the  flash  crash   that  we  saw  on  the  5th  of  February  this   year.  Bitcoin  briefly  touched  $60,000,   triggering  a  massive  $2.67  67  billion  in   liquidations  in  a  single  day.  But  that   was  just  a  macroeconomic  shakeout.  A   contentious  hard  fork  would  trigger  a   liquidity  vacuum  that  would  make  the   February  crash  look  like  a  minor  dip.   Leveraged  longs  would  get  liquidated.   Forced  selling  would  hit  a  thin  order   book  and  the  price  would  nose  dive.  It   is  a  self-reinforcing  machine  of   absolute  carnage.  And  speaking  of   staying  ahead  of  contagion,  this  is   exactly  why  you  need  the  Coin  Bureau   Telegram  channel  in  your  life.  That  is   where  we  share  the  latest  breaking  news,   provide  deep  dive  alpha,  and  give  you   the  most  important  market  updates.  All   pinged  directly  to  your  device  so  that   you  don't  miss  a  thing.  Whether  it's  a   controversial  GitHub  pull  request  or   massive  ETF  outflows,  our  members  see  it   first.  So,  sign  up  today  using  the  link   down  below  or  by  scanning  the  QR  code  on   the  screen.  Now,  before  you  let  this   scenario  send  your  stress  levels  to  the   moon,  consider  that  the  Bitcoin   community  did  not  let  this  proposal  get   very  far.  The  poll  request  was   automatically  closed  by  GitHub  within   roughly  17  hours  and  blocked  a  spam.   Prominent  Bitcoin  Core  contributors  like   Matt  Coralo  and  Peter  Woola  quickly   pointed  out  that  GitHub  wasn't  even  the   appropriate  forum  for  this.  Carpellis   hadn't  even  submitted  a  formal  Bitcoin   improvement  proposal.  And  we  also  have   historical  precedent  that  a  network  can   survive  a  recovery  fork.  Cast  your  mind   back  to  July  of  2016  when  the  Ethereum   network  was  hardforked  to  recover   roughly  50  to  60  million  stolen  in  the   DAO  attack.  Ethereum  survived.  Ethereum   Classic  was  born  and  the  sky  didn't   fall.  So  you  could  argue  that  the  system   worked  perfectly  here.  The  social   consensus  of  Bitcoin  rejected  the  change   immediately,  proving  that  the  network  is   as  immutable  as  we  all  hoped.  But  there   is  a  harder  question  that  the  17-hour   shutdown  is  actually  masking.  Because   the  real  threat  isn't  the  code.  The  real   threat  is  the  precedent  sets  for  the   $89.5  billion  currently  sitting  in  US   spot  Bitcoin  ETFs.  Since  these  ETFs   launched  in  early  2024,  the  plumbing  of   Bitcoin  has  become  deeply  entangled  with   Wall  Street.  Black  Rockck's  IBIT  alone   holds  over  50.15  billion  in  Bitcoin,   representing  roughly  56%  of  the  entire   US  spot  ETF  market.  And  if  you  dig  into   the  legal  prospectus  for  IBIT,  you'll   find  a  rather  worrying  clause.  It  states   that  in  the  event  of  a  hard  fork,  the   sponsor  shall  determine  which  network   shall  constitute  the  Bitcoin  network.   The  sponsor  is  of  course  BlackRock.   Fidelity  and  Bitwise  have  similar   discretionary  language  in  their  filings.   This  means  that  in  a  contentious  fork   scenario,  it  isn't  just  the  miners  or   the  node  operators  who  decide  the  winner   anymore.  It's  Larry  Frink.  If  Black   Rockck  declares  that  the  forked  chain  is   the  real  Bitcoin,  50  billion  in   institutional  capital  instantly  follows   that  decision.  So,  Wall  Street  has   effectively  become  the  kingmaker  in  any   future  governance  dispute  and  that   fundamentally  breaks  the  decentralized   consensus  model.  Furthermore,  if  a  hard   fork  is  ever  successfully  executed  to   recover  stolen  funds,  it  completely   destroys  Bitcoin's  legal  shield.  In   November  2024,  a  US  appeals  court  ruled   that  the  tornado  cash  smart  contracts   could  not  be  sanctioned  by  OFAC  because   they  were  immutable.  Because  no  one   could  alter  the  code,  it  couldn't  be   classified  as  controllable  property.   Immutability  is  literally  the  legal   defense  against  government  seizure.  If   the  Bitcoin  community  proves  that  it  can   rewrite  the  ledger  for  Mount  Gaus   victims,  regulators  will  immediately   demand  the  exact  same  treatment  for   sanctioned  addresses.  The  digital  gold   thesis  relies  entirely  on  the  premise   that  no  CEO,  no  developer,  and  no   government  can  change  the  rules.  If  that   illusion  is  shattered,  well,  the   institutional  capital  will  flee.  And  we   are  already  seeing  how  skittish  the   capital  can  be  with  US  spot  ETFs   suffering  roughly  6.39  billion  in   outflows  over  the  last  few  months.   >> What's  the  verdict?   >> Well,  let's  be  blunt.  The  immediate   threat  is  over.  The  Corpelli's  proposal   was  poorly  executed,  submitted  to  the   wrong  forum,  and  shut  down  in  record   time.  Bitcoin's  technical  robustness   remains  completely  intact,  but  the   vulnerability  this  exposed  is  absolutely   real.  The  fact  that  a  one-time  exception   was  seriously  drafted  for  a  $5.2  billion   honeypot,  proves  that  the  temptation  to   rewrite  history,  however  apparently   well-intended,  will  never  truly  go  away.   The  legal  architecture  of  the  spot  ETFs   has  quietly  shifted  an  enormous  amount   of  governance  power  into  the  hands  of   traditional  finance.  And  the   psychological  damage  of  realizing  how   fragile  social  consensus  can  be  is   profound.  Bitcoin  is  only  as  immutable   as  its  community  wants  it  to  be.  This   time,  the  community  held  the  line.  But   with  $5.2  billion  on  the  table,  you   could  be  certain  this  won't  be  the  last   time  that  this  line  is  tested.  But  what   do  you  think?  Was  the  community  right  to   aggressively  shut  down  the  Mount  Gox   hardfork,  or  should  exceptions  be  made   to  recover  billions  in  stolen  funds?  Let   me  know  your  thoughts  in  the  comments   down  below.  And  if  you  want  to   understand  how  institutional  ETF  flows   are  impacting  Bitcoin's  base  layer,   well,  you  could  check  out  our  video  on   that  right  over  here.  And  if  you  want  to   see  how  macroeconomic  forces  like   tariffs  are  threatening  the  digital  gold   narrative,  well,  you  could  check  out   that  video  right  over  here.  Thank  you   all  so  much  for  watching  and  I'll  see   you  again  very  soon.  This  is  Lewis   signing  off.
summary_hu: A videó elemzése rávilágít arra, hogy Mark Carpellis, a Mount Gox volt vezérigazgatója, megpróbálta megváltoztatni a Bitcoin blockchain történelmét egy hard fork javaslatával, hogy visszaszerezze az elveszett alapokat. Ez a javaslat komoly fenyegetést jelentett a Bitcoin decentralizált eszméjére, mivel a Wall Street, különösen a BlackRock, a jövőbeni döntések fontos szereplőjévé válhat. A Bitcoin közösség azonban hatékonyan elutasította a javaslatot, ami bizonyította a hálózat megmaradását, de a potenciaági veszélyek megmaradtak. Az ETF-ek jogi kerete és a burkolt hatalomátadás a hagyományos pénzügyek irányába változó mércévé válhat, és jövőbeli döntő szakaszokat eredményezhet a Bitcoin történetében. A társadalmi konszenzus törékenysége pedig előrevetíti a jövőbeli kihívásokat.
summary_en: The analysis of the video reveals that Mark Carpellis, former CEO of Mount Gox, proposed a hard fork to rewrite Bitcoin's blockchain history to recover lost funds. This proposal posed a significant threat to Bitcoin's decentralized ideals as Wall Street, particularly BlackRock, could become a key player in future decisions. However, the Bitcoin community swiftly rejected the proposal, proving the network's resilience, yet the underlying vulnerabilities remain. The legal framework surrounding ETFs and the subtle transfer of governance power to traditional finance could pave the way for decisive moments in Bitcoin's future. Furthermore, the fragility of social consensus foreshadows future challenges.
key_points_hu: Mark Carpellis javaslata egy hard fork-ra a Bitcoin történetének megváltoztatására
A Bitcoin közösség sikeresen elutasította a javaslatot
A Wall Street befolyása a Bitcoin jövőjére és decentralizáltságára
key_points_en: Mark Carpellis proposed a hard fork to alter Bitcoin's history
The Bitcoin community successfully rejected the proposal
Wall Street's influence on Bitcoin's future and decentralization
main_topics: Impact of institutional investment on Bitcoin
